meta.title: W460 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W460 engine — OM616, OM617, OM602, M102, M115, M110. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for the original G‑Class. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes G‑Class W460 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W460 engine typically costs £1,500–£4,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM616 diesel sits at £1,500–£2,800, while the M110 straight‑six ranges up to £4,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W460 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM617 five‑cylinder diesel (300 GD) — legendary for its durability, with documented examples exceeding 500,000 miles. The OM616 four‑cylinder diesel is equally robust but less powerful. Both are mechanical, simple, and nearly indestructible with basic maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes G‑Class W460?

acceptedAnswer.text: Bodywork corrosion (bulkhead, door bottoms, wheel arches) is the single biggest issue — not the engine. Mechanical issues include injector pump wear on the OM616/OM617 diesel, K‑Jetronic injection problems on the M110 straight‑six, and carburettor wear on the early M115 four‑cylinder.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes G‑Class W460 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engines are among the most reliable ever built — especially the OM616 and OM617 diesels. However, the W460 is a 40+ year old vehicle. Bodywork, electrics, and suspension components are the real reliability concerns. The engine will outlast the chassis if maintained properly.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W460 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the M115 carburettor (pre‑1982 230 G) is the least refined and thirstiest. The M110 straight‑six is the costliest to maintain. All are fundamentally reliable with proper care.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W460 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Hard starting, excessive smoke, or reduced power (diesel injector pump wear), rough running or hesitation (M110 K‑Jetronic), poor cold starting (M115 carburettor), or a knocking bottom end (any engine) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes G‑Class W460 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the body condition. W460 values are rising — a well‑kept 300 GD can command £20,000+. A £3,000 OM617 recon is 15% of that — safe. Against a tired, rusty £8,000 W460, the same recon is 38% — still viable. The real question is the bodywork. Our 70% Mercedes threshold rarely applies to classic W460s with appreciating values.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W460 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM617 diesel can exceed 500,000 miles with proper servicing. The OM616 is similarly durable. The OM602 five‑cylinder is almost as robust. The M110 straight‑six and M102 four‑cylinder petrols are capable of 200,000+ miles but require more frequent maintenance of the fuel injection systems.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W460, W461 and W463?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three are G‑Class generations. The W460 is the original (this page, 1979–1992), the W461 is the military/utility variant (continued after 1992), and the W463 is the luxury‑oriented model (1990–2018). The W460 has simpler mechanicals, fewer electronics, and the most rugged reputation. Costs vary by generation — see each model's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W460 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, and on the engine block itself (usually on the left side of the block). Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes G‑Class W460 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the original G‑Wagen — from the petrol M115 four‑cylinder to the M110 straight‑six, and the OM616/OM617/OM602 diesel family that built this off‑roader's legendary reputation. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the engine that defined military‑grade dependability. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes G‑Class W460 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the original G‑Class specifically — from the carburetted 230G to the fuel‑injected 280GE, and the diesel range that powered military and civilian G‑Wagens across the globe.

overview.intro: The W460 is the original G‑Class, launched in 1979 as a joint venture between Daimler‑Benz and Steyr‑Daimler‑Puch. Designed initially for military use, it quickly gained civilian appeal thanks to its rugged body‑on‑frame construction, live axles, and three fully lockable differentials — a combination that made it one of the most capable off‑road vehicles ever built. Produced at the Steyr‑Puch factory in Graz, Austria, the W460 was offered in two wheelbases (2400mm and 2850mm) and three body styles (short‑wheelbase wagon, long‑wheelbase wagon, and convertible). The engine range expanded from four initial options to include fuel‑injected petrol and five‑cylinder diesel variants before the W463 luxury successor arrived in 1990.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: W460 (Mercedes‑Benz G‑Class, first generation) • Predecessor: None (G‑Class launched as new model) • Successor: W463 (second generation, luxury‑oriented) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W461 (military/utility variant, continued after W460 ended)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W460 G‑Class generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM616/OM617 diesel and M102 petrol variants as these classic off‑roaders enter their fifth decade of service.

items.quote: “The 3.0‑litre five‑cylinder naturally aspirated diesel is the G‑Class's most legendary engine — indestructible, mechanical, and capable of exceeding 500,000 miles with basic maintenance. 88 PS won't win any races, but it'll get you anywhere.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want the ultimate classic G‑Class diesel with the strongest reputation.

“The 2.8‑litre straight‑six is the most refined petrol in the W460 range — smooth, torquey, and with Bosch K‑Jetronic mechanical fuel injection that's simpler than modern electronic systems. 156 PS transforms the G‑Class from agricultural to genuinely usable.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want the best‑driving W460 petrol without the carburettor's cold‑start compromises.

“The carburettor four‑cylinder is the oldest engine in the range — simple but thirsty, cold‑start sensitive, and with carburettor wear that can be difficult to diagnose. The M102 fuel‑injected replacement was a significant improvement.” Who it’s for: Purists who want the original G‑Class experience and don't mind the compromises.

“The straight‑six is robust, but parts are increasingly expensive and specialist‑only. Timing chain tensioner wear, K‑Jetronic injection system issues, and the sheer size of the engine make it the costliest petrol to maintain. A full rebuild is a four‑figure event.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want six‑cylinder refinement and have the budget to maintain it.

“The 2.4‑litre four‑cylinder diesel is the entry‑level G‑Class engine — 72 PS is slow even by 1980s standards, but it's mechanically simple, cheap to rebuild, and will outlast the chassis. Parts are widely available across Europe.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want a diesel G‑Class on a tighter budget.

“Often overshadowed by the legendary OM617, the later 2.5‑litre five‑cylinder OM602 is actually more refined and almost as durable — 84 PS with a smoother power delivery and lower fuel consumption. It's the sweet spot of the W460 diesel range.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want five‑cylinder diesel refinement without the OM617's classic‑tax premium.

economicsRule.title: The W460 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000 OM617 recon against a £15,000 W460 sits at 20% — safe. Against a tired £8,000 W460, the same recon is 38% — still viable. The real cost of W460 ownership isn't the engine — it's the bodywork. Rust is the W460's biggest enemy. Value the chassis and body first, then the engine. Our 70% Mercedes threshold rarely applies to classic W460s with rising values.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W460 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM616 (240 GD) — simple, cheap parts, indestructible four‑cylinder diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W460 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M110 (280 GE) — six‑cylinder complexity, K‑Jetronic issues, and parts availability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W460 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM617 (300 GD) — legendary reputation, strong demand, and proven durability.

problems.title: Rust & Bodywork Corrosion
problems.rootCause: The W460's boxy body panels and chassis are prone to corrosion. Key areas include the bulkhead (under the windscreen), door bottoms, rear wheel arches, sills, and the chassis around the rear axle mounts. This is the single biggest factor affecting W460 viability — not the engine.
problems.recommendation: Before spending any money on a W460 engine, get the bodywork and chassis thoroughly inspected — a £1,000–£3,000 rust repair is far cheaper than the £5,000–£10,000 restoration that follows a neglected bulkhead or chassis failure.

problems.title: OM616 / OM617 Injector Pump Wear
problems.rootCause: The mechanical injection pump on OM616 and OM617 engines wears over time, causing hard starting, reduced power, and increased smoke. The pre‑chamber design can also suffer from carbon buildup.
problems.recommendation: If your W460 diesel develops hard starting or excessive smoke, get the injection system inspected — a £400–£1,000 pump rebuild is far cheaper than the £1,800–£3,500 replacement that follows a failed pump.

cta.label: Compare OM617 engine prices for your W460 300 GD →

problems.title: M110 K‑Jetronic Injection System Issues
problems.rootCause: The Bosch K‑Jetronic mechanical fuel injection system on the M110 straight‑six can develop issues with age — fuel distributor wear, warm‑up regulator failure, and vacuum leaks are the most common. Diagnosis requires specialist knowledge.
problems.recommendation: If your 280 GE develops rough running, poor cold start, or hesitation, find a K‑Jetronic specialist immediately — a £600–£1,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,200–£4,000 replacement that follows a failed fuel distributor.

cta.label: Compare M110 engine prices for your W460 280 GE →

problems.title: M115 Carburettor Wear (Pre‑1982 230 G)
problems.rootCause: The Solex carburettor on early M115 engines wears with age — throttle shaft play, worn jets, and degraded diaphragms cause cold‑start issues, hesitation, and poor fuel economy.
problems.recommendation: If your early 230 G suffers from poor cold starting or hesitation, get the carburettor rebuilt — a £300–£800 rebuild is far cheaper than the £1,500–£2,800 replacement that follows a failed carburettor.

cta.label: Compare M115 engine prices for your W460 230 G →

replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline figures — W460 shares engine architecture with other Mercedes classics, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W460 G‑Class only. W461 military/utility variants and W463 luxury variants share some engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages.

liveFeed.issue: Lack of power, injector pump wear
